content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Arthur Forcier 
11783 Laurelcrest Drive 
studio City, CA 
91604 
Dear Mr. Forcier: 
March 13, 1989 
Re: 
Your Request For Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-89-058 
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions 
of the Political Reform Act.ll 
Because your request does not 
indicate that you are seeking advice on behalf of a person who has 
duties under the Act, we consider your request to be for informal 
assistance.21 
Therefore, this letter will not address the 
hypothetical questions you presented, but will provide general 
guidance. 
We cannot provide specific answers to hypothetical 
questions because the answers to those questions often will differ 
depending on the facts presented. 
You have asked whether it is permissible for individuals, 
organizations or committees to conduct independent campaigns to 
support or oppose a candidate, and whether particular involvement 
of a candidate in the activities of a committee would affect the 
committee's independent status. 
In addition, you asked whether 
members of an "independent committee" can advise a candidate 
concerning the candidate's campaign expenditures. 
There is nothing in the Act which would prohibit an 
individual, organization or committee from making independent 
expenditures to support or oppose a candidate. 
There is no 
II Government Code section 81000-91015. 
All statutory references 
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission 
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 
18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
21 
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the 
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. 
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. sections 
18329 (c) (3) .) 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 


Arthur Forcier 
Page Twe 
definitien .of "independent cemmittee" in the Act. 
The Act dees 
previde a definitien .of "centrelled cemmittee." 
Fer purpeses .of 
this letter, we use the term "independent cemmittee" te refer te a 
cemmittee which is net a "centrelled cemmittee." 
sectien 82016 prevides: 
"Centrelled cemmittee" means a cemmittee which is 
centrelled directly .or indirectly by a candidate .or 
state measure prepenent .or which acts jeintly with a 
candidate, centrelled cemmittee .or state measure 
prepenent in cennectien with the making .of expenditures. 
A candidate .or state measure prepenent centrels a cemmittee 
if he, his agent .or any ether cemmittee he centrels has 
significant influence en the actiens erdecisiens .of the 
cemmittee. 
The Cemmissien has interpreted the definitien .of "centrelled 
cemmittee" very breadly te include any significant participatien 
in the actiens .of a cemmittee by a candidate, his .or her agent, .or 
representatives .of any ether cemmittee he .or she centrels. 
Attached fer yeur guidance are the fell ewing advice letters issued 
by the Cemmissien en the subject .of what censtitutes "centrel" .of 
a cemmittee: 
Pastrick Advice Letter, Ne. A-87-063 
Fergusen Advice Letter, Ne. A--86-044 
Madden Advice Letter, Ne. A-85-197 
Meme Te Advice File, Ne. T-85-139 
Meme Te Advice File, Ne. M-84-257 
Gress Advice Letter, Ne. A-84-143 
with regard te the making .of "independent expenditures" in 
suppert .of a candidate by an individual, erganizatien .or 
cemmittee, Sectien 82031 prevides: 
"Independent expenditure" means an expenditure 
fer a cemmunicatien made by a persen in cennectien 
with a cemmunicatien which expressly advecates the 
electien .or defeat .of a clearly identified candidate 
.or the qualificatien, passage .or defeat .of a clearly 
identified measure, .or taken as a whele and in centext, 
unambigueusly urges a particular result in an electien 
but which is net made te .or at the behest .of the 
affected candidate .or cemmittee. 


Arthur Forcier 
Page Three 
A payment which is "made at the behest" of a candidate or 
committee is not an "independent expenditure." 
(Section 82031.) 
It is a "contribution" to the candidate or committee. 
(Section 
82011.) 
A payment is "made at the behest" of a candidate or 
committee if it is made: 
.•• under the control or at the direction of, in 
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert 
with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate, 
controlled committee, ... 
(Regulation 18215, copy enclosed.) 
If you have any questions about this letter, please call me 
at (916) 322-5662. 
encls. 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel

Division Chief 
Technical Assistance and 
Analysis Division 


Fair Political Practices Committee 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
To Whom It May Concern: 
Arthur Foricer 
11783 Laurelcrest Dr. 
Studio City, CA 91604 
January 24, 1989 
Do California's present election laws permit individuals or organizations or P ACS to conduct independent 
campaigns in support of or in opposition to a candidate? 
If a candidate is a member of the executive board that controls a PAC, can the PAC legally say it's 
running a campaign that is independent of the candidate, or is the fact that the candidate is an executive 
board member automatically create a situation of non-independence? 
Can members of an independent PAC running an independent campaign also advise the candidate as 
to the expenditures that should be made by the candidate's own controlled PAC? 
If it is legally possible to run an independent campaign in support of a candidate, despite the candidate 
being an executive board member, would any of the following circumstances affect the interpretation 
concerning the independence factor?: 
1) 
10% of the executive board members are significant members of the candidate's campaign. 
2) 
The candidate doesn't attend executive board meetings. 
3) 
The candidate significant campaign supporters don't attend executive board meetings. 
4) 
The executive board appropriates money for an independent campaign but has the organization 
staff determine how the money is to be spent without guidance from the candidate or the executive 
board and without the candidate or the executive board knowing how the money has been spent. 
The independent PAC and the candidate are both involved in a Spring 1989 election. The filing date 
has already past. Neither the candidate nor the independent PAC wish to violate any campaign rules. 
It would be very much appreciated if you would also send any pamphlets, papers, or manuals concerning 
existing election laws and interpretations as well as the answer to the specific questions. Thank you very 
much. 
Sincerely, 
'1/v{:/,-~ ,;7) t~ 
Arthur Forcier 


Fair Political Practices Committee 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804

California

Practices Commission 
Arthur Forcier 
11783 Laurelcrest Drive 
Studio city, CA 
91604 
Dear Mr. Forcier: 
January 31, 1989 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-058 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on January 30, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to the 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
JP:plh 
Very truly yours,
